blog.cta_text: Get weekly insights on smart borrowing strategies

scenes.voiceover_script: When you borrow money, lenders face a simple problem: what if you don't pay back? Their solution? They want something of yours to hold. But here's what most people don't realize: not all collateral is created equal. Some assets make lenders feel safe. Others make them nervous.

Collateral is an asset you pledge as security for a loan. If you stop paying, the lender can seize it. Good collateral has three qualities: it's easy to value, it holds its worth over time, and it's easy to sell. Cars, equipment, and inventory can work. But real estate? That's the gold standard.

Why does real estate dominate? Property values are relatively stable and well-documented. Banks typically lend sixty to seventy percent of home value, leaving room for price drops. And when people risk losing their home, they prioritize that payment. That's why mortgage default rates stay low even during economic stress.

This matters if you're seeking a loan. Have a home with equity? You'll get better rates and higher limits. Trying to borrow against a car or business inventory? Expect tougher terms. Understanding this helps you negotiate smarter and know what lenders really want.

Before borrowing, assess your collateral options. First, calculate your home equity: current value minus what you owe. Second, consider how quickly you need funds. Home equity loans take longer but offer better rates. Third, never pledge assets you can't afford to lose. That vacation property might secure a loan, but default means losing it.
Want to make smarter borrowing decisions? Follow MoneyWise for weekly tips on using the financial system to your advantage. Your assets, your leverage.
